
### Discussion {#discussion}
Our results clearly show that ART fails across a wide range of experimental configurations and distributional settings. They confirm the findings of Lüpsen [-@luepsen:2017; -@luepsen:2018], while providing a clearer picture that ART’s shortcomings are not simply due to issues in the interpretation of main or interaction effects, but reflect more fundamental limitations of the method.

A natural question arises: several other studies have evaluated ART, yet none has reported such problems. In particular, the studies by @Mansouri:1995 and @elkin:2021 were extensive and concluded that, aside from the problematic Cauchy distribution, ART maintains correct Type I error rates. Why, then, do our results differ so markedly?

A careful examination of these studies reveals that they considered a relatively limited set of conditions under which ART happens to perform well. Specifically:

1. @Mansouri:1995 and @elkin:2021 did not evaluate discrete distributions, which explains why they did not observe ART's failures with ordinal, binomial, or Poisson data, even when all effects are null.

2. @Mansouri:1995 focused on data generated from the naive model shown in @fig-higgins-model, which assumes linear relationships, equal variances, and continuous responses. Under these restrictive conditions, ART appears to behave correctly. We also note that @Mansouri:1995 evaluated only interactions and did not consider main effects.

3. @elkin:2021 evaluated generalized linear models with continuous responses but considered only scenarios in which all effects were null when assessing Type I error rates. This prevented them from detecting ART's confounding of effects. Furthermore, their evaluation did not account for unequal variances.

We also note that, for within-participants designs with log-normal data, ART still exhibits a mild inflation of Type I error rates even when all effects are null, reaching up to $9.86\%$ for the 3 $\times$ 3 $\times$ 3 within-subject design. This effect is more pronounced in smaller samples. While @elkin:2021 do not report this issue explicitly, a closer inspection of [their supplementary materials](https://zenodo.org/records/5563367) reveals inflated Type I error rates for one-factor contrasts under log-normal data (particularly for their 2 $\times$ 2 $\times$ 2 designs), even when using their contrast-corrected ART procedure. These results are consistent with our findings.  

The results by @Payton:2006 also deserve attention, as their study is one of the few to evaluate ART on discrete data, focusing on interaction effects. They compared several transformation methods, including ART, on count data following a Poisson distribution in a 2 $\times$ 2 design. Their results showed that standard ANOVA without transformation (PAR) performed best, maintaining Type I error rates close to $5\%$, even in the presence of strong main effects on both factors. ART also performed well, with only a mild error inflation (around 6$\%$-6.5$\%$). At first glance, these findings appear to contradict our results (see @fig-ratio-interaction-2 and @fig-designs-interaction-2). 

However, since @Payton:2006 do not provide detailed information about the distributions used in their simulations, it is difficult to pinpoint the exact source of this discrepancy. One plausible explanation is that their simulations involved count data with a wide range of values and low skewness. Such distributions may approximate normality, in which case both standard ANOVA and ART would be expected to perform well. This highlights a central limitation of ART: it tends to behave well in situations where standard ANOVA is already appropriate, but it introduces greater risks when violations of ANOVA's assumptions become pronounced. This suggests that ART offers no practical benefit as a general-purpose method.
